rulings.Case Title: JOSEPH LUEBBERKE VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Pltf'S Mental Examination
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, March 7, 2013, Line 2, DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO's Motion For Plaintiff's Mental Examination. Pro Tem Judge Ernst Halperin, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: HEARING REQUIRED. The court finds that good cause exists for granting the City's motion for a mental examination of Mr. Luebberke. However, the City has not identified all of the tests Dr. Berg proposes to utilized. Any order granting a motion for the mental examination of a plaintiff must name the person who shall perform the evaluation, set forth the specific conditions for the examination, and specifically identify all of the potential diagnostic tests and procedures to be used in the examination. Carpenter v. Superior Court (2006) 141 Cal. App. 4th 249, 267. The parties are to meet and confer prior to the hearing concerning the protocol for the examination. To the extent they cannot agree on whether a particular test is appropriate, the parties are to be prepared to provide evidence or argument sufficient to allow the court to make a determination concerning that test. Plaintiff's argument that he is entitled to discovery concerning Dr. Berg's prior engagements before submitting to the examination is not well taken. That information may be relevant to Dr. Berg's credibility as a testifying expert, but is not a basis for precluding the examination. Plaintiff may make an audio-recording of the examination, and after the examination, the City shall provide Plaintiff's counsel with copies of any written materials concerning the examinations, written responses by plaintiff, and all notes taken during the examination. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: JAMES W. GAY VS. CREATIVE ALLIANCE, A CALIFORNIA LIMITED LIABILTY et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff James Gay'S Ntc Of Motion To Compel Further Responses To Pltf Requests For Docs
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, March 7, 2013, Line 7, Plaintiff James Gay's Motion To Compel Further Responses To Plaintiff's Requests For Documents. Pro Tem Judge Ernst Halperin, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: GRANTED. Within 15 days of the date of this Order, Defendant WB Coyle, Danilo Design Group, L.P., and Telegraph Hill Properties, Inc. shall each: (1) provide further responses to Plaintiff's requests for production of documents - set one, (2) produce all responsive documents, and (3) serve plaintiff with privilege logs identifying with particularity any responsive documents withheld on the ground of privilege as well as the basis for the privilege claimed. See C.C.P. 2031.240(c)(effective January 1, 2013). Given that Defendants have withdrawn their objections based upon privacy, no documents shall be withheld on the ground of privacy. The request for sanctions is DENIED. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to ernst@ernsthalperin.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: JOSEPH CAMILLOCCI VS. SAINT FRANCIS MEMORIAL HOSPITAL, A CALIFORNIA et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant'S Motion To Establish The Sequence Of Discovery
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, March 7, 2013, Line 22, Defendant'S Motion To Establish The Sequence Of Discovery Hearing required. The court finds that specific circumstances justify the sua sponte appointment of a referee. (CCP § 639(a)(5).) It is evident from the number of outstanding and pending discovery issues that significant differences exist between the parties. Besides the two instant motions, the parties have the following pending discovery motions: Motion to Quash Overbroad Subpoenas, Motions for Order Compelling Further Responses to First Request for Production of Documents, Motion for Court to Require Plaintiff's Consent to Disclosure of Documents, and Motion to Quash in Whole or Part Defendants' Overbroad Record Subpoenas. The two motions involving production requests were continued twice in order for the parties to meet and confer to narrow the outstanding issues and to file to a joint separate statements. For the hearing on February 5, 2013, their last joint separate statements were 929 pages (for the production requests propounded on Dignity Health) and 267 pages (for the production requests propounded on Saint Francis Memorial Hospital). All of the present motions involve the first round of discovery, and have arisen since the Court issued its first discovery order on June 12, 2012. At this point, there have been three resolved motions, four unresolved motions, including the two instant motions, and two new pending motions. Resolution of these matters has consumed and will continue to consume an inordinate amount of judicial resources. Therefore, the court is inclined to appoint a discovery referee. The parties should meet and confer in an effort to agree on a referee, but if they are unable to do so, each side should identify up to three prospective referees. Plaintiff shall be prepared to present evidence regarding his ability to pay the referee costs. The court will not provide a court reporter for this discovery hearing. If any party wishes a reporter, the parties must meet and confer and agree on a single reporter. =302/MJM

rulings.Case Title: JOSEPH CAMILLOCCI VS. SAINT FRANCIS MEMORIAL HOSPITAL, A CALIFORNIA et al

The court finds that specific circumstances justify the sua sponte appointment of a referee. (CCP § 639(a)(5).) It is evident from the number of outstanding and pending discovery issues that significant differences exist between the parties. Besides the two instant motions, the parties have the following pending discovery motions: Motion to Quash Overbroad Subpoenas, Motions for Order Compelling Further Responses to First Request for Production of Documents, Motion for Court to Require Plaintiff's Consent to Disclosure of Documents, and Motion to Quash in Whole or Part Defendants' Overbroad Record Subpoenas. The two motions involving production requests were continued twice in order for the parties to meet and confer to narrow the outstanding issues and to file to a joint separate statements. For the hearing on February 5, 2013, their last joint separate statements were 929 pages (for the production requests propounded on Dignity Health) and 267 pages (for the production requests propounded on Saint Francis Memorial Hospital). All of the present motions involve the first round of discovery, and have arisen since the Court issued its first discovery order on June 12, 2012. At this point, there have been three resolved motions, four unresolved motions, including the two instant motions, and two new pending motions. Resolution of these matters has consumed and will continue to consume an inordinate amount of judicial resources. Therefore, the court is inclined to appoint a discovery referee. The parties should meet and confer in an effort to agree on a referee, but if they are unable to do so, each side should identify up to three prospective referees. If a party believes they are unable to pay referee costs, this shall be addressed at the hearing. The court will not provide a court reporter for this discovery hearing. If any party wishes a reporter, the parties must meet and confer and agree on a single reporter. =302/MJM

rulings.Case Title: RAHMON MOMOH VS. CALIFORNIA PUBLIC UTILITIES COMMISSION et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Interrogatoriers And For Sanctions; Memo
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, March 7, 2013, Line 5, PLAINTIFF RAHMON MOMOH's Motion To Compel Further Responses To Interrogatories And For Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge Ernst Halperin, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff Momoh's motion to compel is GRANTED IN PART as follows. Defendant CPUC shall respond to special interrogatory number 4 and special interrogatory number 5 with respect to the following subset of people, "Candidates for promotion to PURA-III through PURA-V positions from January 1, 2008 through December 31, 2011. Candidates means those persons who applied for positions" The information sought is relevant to the allegation in Plaintiff's Second Amended Complaint at Paragraph 41 that "Defendant's promotional processes had a disparate impact on applicants over 40 years of age, including Plaintiff, and the allegations at Paragraphs 6 that, "As a result of the CPUC's design of its promotional/recruitment system to disfavor older, experienced employees, CPUC managers and supervisors responsible for evaluating candidates were enabled to discriminate against candidates on the basis of race, color, national origin." The requests of both parties for sanctions are DENIED. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to ernst@ernsthalperin.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: ABRAHAM WELDEMARIAM VS. WALGREENS HOME CARE INC., AN ILLINOIS CORPORATION et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion For Dismissal And Entry Of Judgment For Failure To Timely Amend
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, March 7, 2013, Line 14, DEFENDANT P&G - CLAIROL INC.,, THE PROCTER AND GAMBLE U.S. BUSINESS SERVICES Notice Of Motion For Dismissal And Entry Of Judgment For Failure To Timely Amend Granted. No opposition filed. The Complaint is dismissed with prejudice as to Defendants P&G-Clairol, Inc. and The Proctor & Gamble U.S. Business Services Company pursuant to Civil Procedure Code section 581(f). Defendants shall prepare and submit a proposed judgment. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: AVERY MROZINSKI, BY HER GUARDIAN AD LITEM, TODD et al VS. OLD NAVY, LLC, A DELAWARE CORPORATION et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Dismiss Or Stay The Action
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, March 7, 2013, Line 15, DEFENDANT OLD NAVY, LLC Notice Of Motion To Dismiss Or Stay The Action The motion is denied. Defendant fails to show that California is an inconvenient forum. The balance of public and private interests does not show that it would be more just to have this case adjudicated in Michigan. Defendant is headquartered in California and therefore, a California forum is presumptively convenient. (Stangvik v. Shiley, 54 Cal. 3d 744, 760). The alleged wrongful conduct (approval of the T-stands) allegedly occurred in California. The instant case is not complex and will not unduly burden this Court's calendar. The key witnesses (plaintiffs) are willing to resolve this matter in California. Lastly, ["t]here thus must be evidence-not merely bald assertions-to support the trial court's determination." (Ford Motor Co. v. Insurance Co. of North America (1995) 35 Cal.App.4th 604. Here defendant merely relies on the allegations of the amended complaint to support its motion. The amended complaint does not clearly show that crucial evidence or witnesses are in Michigan. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: PATRIZ REGALADO VS. CORE STUDIO SPX LLC, et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Motion And Motion For Preliminary Injunction
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, March 7, 2013, Line 18, DEFENDANT CHU-HSIN YANG Ntc Of Motion And Motion For Preliminary Injunction Denied. Ms. Yang has failed to show a likelihood of success on the merits or irreparable harm. The evidence tends to show that Ms. Regalado has acted within her authority under the Operating Agreement and in the interests of the LLC. In any event, any harm can be remedied by money damages. Further, as alleged in her Cross-Complaint, Ms. Yang may pursue an election to purchase membership interest pursuant to Corp. Code 17351(b). Ms. Yang has not met her burden of showing that a preliminary injunction is necessary in the interim.
